title: Implementing Login
intro: You're adding login to your task manager. Think of it like building a front door — it needs to keep intruders out while letting residents in smoothly. A user reports they can't sign in, and you discover the auth implementation has some fundamental issues. How do you fix it?

teach.title: Building a Login Flow
teach.body: A solid login flow has three parts: a form that collects credentials, server-side validation that checks them, and session management that remembers the user. Each part has common pitfalls — weak validation, missing error handling, or insecure token storage.

teach.title: Common Login Mistakes
teach.code:
BAD: if (password === storedPassword)  // plain text comparison
GOOD: if (await bcrypt.compare(password, hashedPassword))

BAD: res.json({ token, password })  // leaking password in response
GOOD: res.json({ token, user: { id, email } })
teach.annotation: Always hash passwords and never include sensitive data in API responses.

teach.title: Password Hashing Is Like a Shredder
teach.body: Imagine if instead of storing a master key to your house, you stored a shredded version of it. If someone breaks into your key storage, all they find is confetti — useless. But when you bring your actual key, the system can shred it the same way and compare the confetti patterns. That's hashing: the original password is never stored, just its irreversible fingerprint.

A secure login flow is non-negotiable for any app that handles real users. Get this right and you've cleared the biggest trust hurdle — people will enter their email, create accounts, and eventually open their wallets.
Use an auth library or provider instead of building login from scratch. If you must build it, always hash passwords, validate on the server, and never expose sensitive data in responses.

start.narrative: A user reports they can't log in to your task manager. You check the login endpoint and find the password is being compared in plain text, and the error message says 'Incorrect password for user john@example.com'. What's your first priority?

choices.text: Just fix the error message to be less specific
Fix the plain-text password comparison first, then address the error message
Rewrite the entire auth system from scratch

surface-fix.narrative: You change the error to 'Invalid credentials' — better, but passwords are still stored and compared in plain text. A code reviewer reads the diff and asks why the underlying bug is still there. You realize cosmetic fixes don't address the real risk.

choices.text: Add bcrypt hashing on top of the cosmetic fix and keep the message changes
Insist the message change is enough and merge the PR as-is

fix-hashing.narrative: Good call. You implement bcrypt hashing on the signup path and update the error message to a generic 'Invalid email or password'. New accounts are safe — but the database still holds plain-text passwords for existing users. What's your approach?

choices.text: Write a one-off script that bcrypts every existing password row in place, then switch login to bcrypt.compare
Hash each existing password the next time the user logs in successfully, and force-reset everyone who hasn't logged in within 90 days

overhaul.narrative: Rewriting from scratch is risky and slow. Users still can't log in. Your team lead asks why a login bug fix is turning into a two-week project. You scope it down to just fix the critical issues first.

choices.text: Fix hashing and error messages, plan the bigger rewrite for later
Push back and keep the full rewrite on the schedule

migrate-existing.narrative: Passwords are now hashed everywhere. Login authenticates correctly. Now you need to decide how the session lives across requests. Where do you store the session token after a successful login?

choices.text: Set a signed, httpOnly, secure cookie with a short-lived JWT and a refresh token rotation
Store the JWT in localStorage and read it on each fetch
Set a cookie with no expiry and no httpOnly flag — it's simpler

error-handling.narrative: Sessions persist correctly. A user submits the form with an empty email field and gets a 500 error and a stack trace in the browser. How do you tighten the error path?

choices.text: Validate the input on the server, return a 400 with a clean field-level error message, and log the full error server-side only
Add a 'required' attribute on the input and call it done
Wrap the handler in a try/catch that returns 200 with an empty body so the user never sees the error

rate-limit-step.narrative: Sentry shows one IP attempting 800 logins in 10 minutes against the same email. Your endpoint happily processes every one. What do you add?

choices.text: Add per-IP and per-account rate limiting plus a temporary lockout after N failures, with a generic error message
Add a CAPTCHA on the form and assume that stops bots
Block the single IP in your firewall and move on

excellent-end.narrative: Your login flow now hashes passwords with bcrypt, migrated all legacy data, stores sessions in httpOnly cookies, validates input cleanly, and rate-limits brute-force attempts. The original bug is fixed and the surface area around it is hardened.
excellent-end.feedback: This is production-grade auth: real hashing, real session security, clean error contract, and abuse-resistant. The pattern — fix the bug, then audit everything adjacent to it — is what turns one-time fixes into systematic security improvements.

mediocre-end.narrative: Auth is mostly secure, but a CAPTCHA on its own gets bypassed by services that solve them for fractions of a cent. Without rate limiting and lockouts, a determined attacker still works through your accounts at scale.
mediocre-end.feedback: CAPTCHA is a speed bump, not a barrier. Layer it on top of rate limits and account lockouts — defense in depth. A single control is a single point of failure.

weak-end.narrative: You blocked one IP. The attacker rotates through a residential proxy pool and continues unimpeded. Within a day they've credential-stuffed dozens of accounts using passwords leaked from other breaches.
weak-end.feedback: IP-level blocks are trivial to bypass. Assume any attacker has unlimited IPs and design rate limits per account, with progressive lockouts. Block the behavior, not the address.
